title: Foundation: Contracts, Bitemporal Standards, and Publish Boundary
description:
Why this exists:
- The rest of the program fails if contracts, publish semantics, and temporal standards drift across modules.

Deliverables:
- Canonical intelligence contract package under src/contracts/intelligence.
- Schema families for news_intel, intel_pub, and intel_export.
- Lane-run, manifest, pointer, and lifecycle scaffolding.
- Review/metadata API skeletons and bundle export skeleton.

Exclusions:
- No end-user serving logic.
- No stock-screener-owned adapters beyond consumer compatibility fixtures.
design: Sequence this first. Define producer-owned contracts before new lanes or schemas spread through the repo. Preserve the invariant that downstream consumers read only published manifest outputs. Touch src/contracts/intelligence, src/publish, internal API surfaces, migrations, and contract tests. Main risks are contract churn and leaking work-in-progress tables into serving flows.
acceptance_criteria:
- Contract package, publish schemas, lifecycle states, bundle skeleton, and compatibility tests exist.
- Publishable rows can be keyed by manifest and versioned contracts.
- Later workstreams have stable producer-side interfaces to build on.
notes: Treat this as the boundary-setting workstream. If ambiguity arises later, align to the producer/consumer split defined here rather than inventing local exceptions.
close_reason: All 5 child tasks complete and verified. Contract package (version registry, lanes, schemas, ownership, DB schemas, fixtures) under src/contracts/intelligence/. Publish module (service, repository, exporter) under src/publish/. Migration 018 creates 3 PG schemas + 5 tables. Intel API routes at /intel/*. 226 tests across the workstream. All acceptance criteria met: contract package exists, publishable rows are manifest-keyed and contract-versioned, stable producer interfaces are exported. No end-user serving logic or stock-screener adapters.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text:
Background and sequencing rationale:
- This workstream is the program boundary setter. Everything downstream assumes that contracts, manifest semantics, temporal fields, and publish lifecycle rules were decided here instead of being re-litigated in each lane.
- Task order matters: q88.1.1 defines the contract language, q88.1.2 establishes the storage namespaces and lifecycle tables, q88.1.3 adds the run/manifest/pointer skeleton, q88.1.4 exposes minimal producer-owned integration hooks, and q88.1.5 proves the bundle/export contract matches the published contract.
- If a later task feels blocked by a missing schema or contract rule, the fix probably belongs here rather than as a local workaround.
- Main invariants: every publishable object is lineaged, manifest-keyed where appropriate, and compatible with both PostgreSQL and bundle-backed consumption.

title: Define intelligence contract package, semantic versioning, and ownership rules
description:
Deliverable:
- Define the producer-owned contract package under src/contracts/intelligence, including contract version identifiers, lane names, manifest object shapes, and ownership boundaries.

Close criteria:
- Later schema, publish, and bundle tasks can import one canonical contract surface instead of inventing local shapes.
design: Capture the minimum public schema required for producer-side publication and downstream consumption. Include semantic versioning, deprecation intent, and manifest-keyed objects. Preserve the invariant that contract ownership remains in news-tracker and that every published object is attributable to a contract version.
acceptance_criteria:
- Contract package exists with version registry and core schemas.
- Ownership and compatibility rules are documented in-code.
- Contract tests can target one canonical source of truth.
notes: Downstream tasks depend on this to avoid schema drift. Do not let lane-specific modules define their own export contracts ad hoc.
close_reason: Contract package created under src/contracts/intelligence/ with version registry (ContractVersion, ContractRegistry), canonical lane definitions (4 lanes: narrative, filing, structural, backtest), manifest/lineage/published-object schemas (Pydantic), and ownership/compatibility rules. 77 contract tests pass.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: every later lane needs one contract source of truth. Must remain true: producer-owned schemas live in news-tracker and published objects stay manifest-aware. Close evidence: shared contract types/version registry exist and downstream compatibility tests can target them.

title: Add news_intel / intel_pub / intel_export schemas plus run lifecycle tables
description:
Deliverable:
- Add the news_intel, intel_pub, and intel_export schema families plus the foundational lifecycle/state tables needed by the program.

Close criteria:
- Migrations exist for the new schema namespaces and shared lifecycle primitives without breaking current theme/narrative tables.
design: Model the database boundary before lane-specific tables proliferate. Include publish lifecycle states, run metadata anchors, and shared temporal/lineage columns where appropriate. Preserve coexistence with existing tables so migration rollout and replay remain safe.
acceptance_criteria:
- New schema namespaces exist in migrations.
- Shared lifecycle tables are present and testable.
- Existing repo features can coexist with the new schemas during rollout.
notes: This task is schema groundwork, not full read-model population. Favor durable boundaries over premature optimization.
close_reason: Migration 018 creates news_intel/intel_pub/intel_export PostgreSQL schemas with lane_runs, manifests, manifest_pointers, published_objects, and export_bundles tables. Python dataclasses mirror all 5 tables with validation. 109 tests pass (31 new).

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: schema namespaces and lifecycle tables need to exist before lane-specific storage sprawls. Must remain true: new schemas coexist safely with current repo tables. Close evidence: migrations create news_intel/intel_pub/intel_export plus shared lifecycle primitives without breaking existing flows.

title: Implement lane-run, manifest, pointer, and publish-state skeleton
description:
Deliverable:
- Implement the skeleton objects for lane runs, manifests, manifest pointers, and publish states used by all later intelligence lanes.

Close criteria:
- Narrative, filing, structural, and rollup lanes have a common publish scaffold to target.
design: Define the minimal orchestration substrate: lane run identity, manifest composition, pointer advancement targets, and state transitions such as draft, qa_failed, quarantined, published, and superseded. Preserve atomic pointer semantics and avoid tying the model to any single lane.
acceptance_criteria:
- Lane-run and manifest skeletons exist.
- Publish states are explicit and reusable across lanes.
- Pointer semantics can be exercised in tests before real data population.
notes: This task should unblock later publication work without forcing final implementation detail too early.
close_reason: Publish module created under src/publish/ with PublishRepository (CRUD for all 5 intelligence tables) and PublishService (state machines for run lifecycle and publish transitions, atomic pointer advancement, manifest sealing, checksum computation). 60 tests exercise state machines, pointer semantics (isolation, history tracking, lane mismatch rejection), and full lifecycle flows using in-memory mock repository.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: all later lanes need one run/manifest/pointer scaffold. Must remain true: pointer movement is explicit and lane-neutral. Close evidence: publish states, lane-run identities, and manifest skeletons can be exercised in tests before real lane data lands.

title: Add internal metadata/review API skeletons and compatibility fixtures
description:
Deliverable:
- Add internal API skeletons for metadata lookup and review submission paths, plus compatibility fixtures that downstream consumers can test against.

Close criteria:
- Producer-side metadata and review round-trips can be exercised without shipping end-user UX in this repo.
design: Keep the surface narrow and producer-owned: run metadata, review intake, and contract fixture responses. Avoid embedding stock-screener-specific routing concerns; the purpose is to give downstream consumers stable integration targets while preserving ownership in news-tracker.
acceptance_criteria:
- Internal API skeletons exist for metadata and review flows.
- Compatibility fixtures are available for contract tests.
- The skeleton does not introduce product-serving concerns into news-tracker.
notes: The point is integration stability, not a full review UI. Keep it infrastructure-oriented.
close_reason: Internal API routes at /intel/* for run metadata, manifest, pointer, object, and review endpoints. Compatibility fixtures for all contract types. PublishService dependency factory. 30 tests: 16 API route tests (contract info, CRUD, review transitions, error cases) + 14 fixture compatibility tests.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: downstream integration needs stable producer hooks early, but this repo should not grow product UX. Must remain true: review and metadata endpoints stay infrastructure-oriented. Close evidence: internal routes/fixtures support contract tests and review round-trips without product-serving creep.

title: Add bundle export skeleton, checksums, and contract-compat tests
description:
Deliverable:
- Add the first bundle export scaffold, checksum generation, and contract-compat tests for exported artifacts.

Close criteria:
- The repo can prove that PostgreSQL publication and bundle export speak the same contract, even before all lanes are populated.
design: Build a minimal immutable bundle shape around manifest metadata, export artifacts, and integrity checks. Preserve parity with the contract package and use tests to ensure bundle-backed consumers will see the same point-in-time semantics as direct published-table readers.
acceptance_criteria:
- Bundle export skeleton exists.
- Checksums are generated and verifiable.
- Contract-compat tests cover both direct publication and bundle surfaces.
notes: This is the seed of the export boundary. Later tasks should extend it, not replace it with lane-specific bundle logic.
close_reason: Bundle export skeleton with BundleExporter (JSONL serialization, SHA-256 checksums, sealed-manifest gate), pure-function utilities (build_bundle_lines, compute_bundle_checksum, parse_bundle_lines, verify_bundle_checksum), and 23 tests proving contract parity between DB and bundle consumers.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: the export boundary should be proven before dozens of lane-specific objects depend on it. Must remain true: bundle-backed and PostgreSQL-backed consumers see the same contract semantics. Close evidence: checksum generation and parity tests pass against the export skeleton.

title: Evidence Claims and Resolver Cascade
description:
Why this exists:
- The architecture pivots from theme-centric heuristics to evidence claims resolved through conservative entity and relation handling.

Deliverables:
- evidence_claims schema with idempotent keys and lineage rules.
- Deterministic resolver cascade over security master, aliases, and context.
- Controlled LLM fallback.
- Review triggers, reviewer queue backend, and claim governance hooks.

Exclusions:
- No silent promotion of low-confidence, high-impact claims to authoritative state.
design: Layer deterministic resolution ahead of expensive or ambiguous fallbacks. Touch new src/claims modules, security master extensions, review state plumbing, migrations, and quality/dead-letter tests. Preserve the invariant that high-impact edges are reviewable and that unresolved ambiguity becomes queued work rather than hidden data corruption.
acceptance_criteria:
- Claim creation is idempotent, lineaged, and review-aware.
- Resolver tiers are explicit and testable.
- Ambiguous or risky cases route into review/quarantine paths instead of directly mutating authoritative intelligence.
notes: This workstream creates the atomic substrate for everything downstream. If a later task needs truth without claim provenance, the missing claim should be fixed here, not papered over elsewhere.

title: Observability, Governance, and Reliability
description:
Why this exists:
- This program adds multiple new failure modes: missing lineage, stale evidence, parser drift, contract incompatibility, and bad publishes.

Deliverables:
- Metrics for lineage completeness, unresolved entities, filing parse quality, and stale evidence.
- Per-lane tracing, failure-rate dashboards, and freshness budgets.
- Publish/bundle/coverage drift metrics and contract enforcement.
- Replay, quarantine, and operator inspection hooks.
design: Extend existing observability foundations rather than inventing a separate ops surface. Touch src/observability, metrics/tracing integration in each new subsystem, QA checks, and reliability tests. Preserve the invariant that publish and replay safety are observable first-class concerns, not post-launch cleanups.
acceptance_criteria:
- Critical intelligence quality, freshness, and publish metrics exist.
- Failures can be detected, quarantined, and inspected without guesswork.
- New lanes expose the telemetry needed for reliable shadow and production operation.
notes: This workstream starts early but finishes late. Keep it open until the new claims, filings, assertions, and publish machinery all emit meaningful telemetry.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text:
Background and sequencing rationale:
- The new intelligence layer introduces failure modes the current repo does not fully need to track yet: missing lineage, resolver ambiguity drift, filing parse fragility, stale evidence, bundle/export failures, and unsafe manifests.
- Task order matters: q88.11.1 adds the basic quality metrics, q88.11.2 makes lane execution observable, q88.11.3 instruments the publish boundary and coverage drift, and q88.11.4 adds replay/quarantine/operator hooks once the key lanes and publish path are real.
- This workstream starts early but closes late by design.
- Main invariants: quality and release decisions should be evidence-driven, publish safety should be visible operationally, and failures should remain inspectable rather than disappearing into retries.

title: Add lineage completeness, unresolved entity, filing parse, and stale-evidence metrics
description:
Deliverable:
- Add metrics for lineage completeness, unresolved entities, filing parse quality, and stale evidence.

Close criteria:
- Core quality risks in the new intelligence layer are measurable rather than anecdotal.
design: Extend existing observability primitives to capture the failure and quality modes introduced by claims, filings, and publication. Preserve per-lane attribution and make sure missing lineage or unresolved-entity drift can be measured historically.
acceptance_criteria:
- Lineage, unresolved-entity, parse-quality, and stale-evidence metrics exist.
- Metrics are attributable to lanes or subsystems.
- Later QA and cutover work can use these signals directly.
notes: If quality problems cannot be measured, they will become release arguments based on vibes rather than evidence.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: the new pipeline introduces quality risks that do not surface automatically through existing metrics. Must remain true: lineage completeness, unresolved-entity rates, parse quality, and stale evidence are measurable by lane or subsystem. Close evidence: operators can inspect these metrics historically and use them in QA decisions.

title: Add per-lane tracing, failure-rate dashboards, and freshness budgets
description:
Deliverable:
- Add per-lane tracing, failure-rate dashboards, and freshness budgets across the new intelligence pipeline.

Close criteria:
- Operators can see where a lane is failing or aging out without reconstructing state by hand.
design: Build lane-aware traces and dashboards around the new filing, claim, assertion, and publish flows. Preserve end-to-end visibility across worker boundaries and make freshness budgets explicit so stale-but-not-broken lanes are still visible operationally.
acceptance_criteria:
- Per-lane tracing exists.
- Failure rates and freshness budgets are observable.
- Dashboards or equivalent reports surface lane health coherently.
notes: This task is about operational clarity, not decorative dashboards.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: once the new lanes exist, failures and staleness need lane-level operational visibility. Must remain true: tracing and freshness budgets remain end-to-end and not reduced to coarse service uptime. Close evidence: lane-aware traces and health views show where work is failing or aging out.

title: Add publish/bundle/coverage drift metrics and contract-compat enforcement
description:
Deliverable:
- Add publish/bundle/coverage-drift metrics and contract-compat enforcement signals.

Close criteria:
- Publication quality and contract integrity can be monitored continuously instead of only during manual release review.
design: Instrument the publish boundary itself: bundle export health, coverage drift by pack/tier, and contract compatibility checks. Preserve explicit signals around when a manifest should be blocked or quarantined rather than discovering incompatibility after consumers break.
acceptance_criteria:
- Publish, bundle, coverage-drift, and contract-compat metrics exist.
- These signals can block or warn on unsafe publication states.
- Later cutover work can depend on measurable publication health.
notes: The publish boundary deserves its own telemetry because that is where producer mistakes become consumer outages.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: the publish boundary is where producer errors become consumer outages. Must remain true: publish, bundle, coverage-drift, and contract-compat signals remain explicit and actionable. Close evidence: these metrics can block, warn, or quarantine unsafe publication states.

title: Add replay, quarantine, and operator inspection hooks
description:
Deliverable:
- Add replay, quarantine, and operator inspection hooks that make failures inspectable and recoverable.

Close criteria:
- The new intelligence layer can be debugged and safely replayed after bad runs or quarantines.
design: Expose the minimum operator-oriented hooks needed for inspection, quarantine, and replay without turning news-tracker into a second product UI. Preserve linkage from observed failures back to runs, manifests, claims, and filing artifacts so recovery paths are concrete.
acceptance_criteria:
- Replay and quarantine hooks exist.
- Operator inspection can trace failures back to concrete runs and artifacts.
- Recovery paths are testable and auditable.
notes: This task is the practical safety net for running the new pipeline under real load.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: the system needs an operational recovery path before cutover. Must remain true: replay, quarantine, and inspection hooks trace failures back to concrete runs and artifacts. Close evidence: bad runs can be inspected and replayed without guesswork.

title: Assertion Resolution and Derived Edge Models
description:
Why this exists:
- Raw claims are too granular and unstable to act as the serving substrate for graph, scoring, or published outputs.

Deliverables:
- resolved_assertions, support links, contradiction links, and validity windows.
- Aggregation logic over support, freshness, diversity, and review state.
- Assertion-derived edge/history/exposure/path-cache inputs.
- Recompute hooks after review decisions.
design: Keep assertions as the stable current-belief layer between claims and every downstream lane. Touch new src/assertions modules, graph adapters, migrations, and assertion/edge recompute tests. Preserve the invariant that derived edges and scores consume assertions rather than raw claims directly, and that contradictions remain explainable rather than discarded.
acceptance_criteria:
- Assertion tables and recompute paths exist.
- Current belief, support, contradiction, and freshness can be queried and explained.
- Downstream graph, scoring, and publication work can depend on assertions as the authoritative intermediate layer.
notes: Treat this as the seam that turns extraction output into publishable intelligence. If graph or scoring logic wants to skip it, the design has drifted.

title: Narrative Lane V2
description:
Why this exists:
- The repo already has narrative momentum infrastructure, but it currently behaves more like a direct product surface than a manifest-published intelligence lane.

Deliverables:
- Reframe narrative runs as input-lane state.
- Introduce narrative_frame/theme_concept mapping.
- Compute attention, corroboration, confirmation, and novelty/persistence components.
- Publish manifest-keyed narrative outputs and rollups.
- Fold the existing kh1 parity/evaluation task into this workstream.
design: Extend src/narrative rather than replacing it wholesale. Touch narrative schemas/repository/worker/CLI/tests plus identity and publish adapters. Preserve replay/backfill parity, avoid breaking current theme behavior during the transition, and keep narrative publication dependent on assertions and manifests instead of ad hoc serving tables.
acceptance_criteria:
- Narrative V2 has explicit component scores and manifest-keyed outputs.
- Existing narrative replay/backfill quality work is integrated into the new lane.
- Narrative becomes a producer-side lane that can be published and backtested cleanly.
notes: This workstream should harvest existing narrative strengths while removing theme-centric assumptions that make the current implementation hard to publish or compare point-in-time.

title: Backtesting, Shadow Evaluation, and Cutover
description:
Why this exists:
- The program is only worth shipping if the new intelligence layer is replay-safe, quantitatively useful, and governable at release time.

Deliverables:
- Point-in-time replay extensions for claims, assertions, filings, lane runs, and manifests.
- Ablation runs across narrative, filing, structure, and divergence layers.
- Shadow-vs-current disagreement sets and QA summaries.
- Publish thresholds, quarantine policy, and cutover checklist.
design: Extend src/backtest and evaluation tooling to operate on the new producer-side substrate rather than old theme-only state. Touch replay services, audit/versioning code, metrics, QA reporting, and release criteria. Preserve no-lookahead behavior, reproducibility, and explicit publish gates before any cutover decisions.
acceptance_criteria:
- Point-in-time replays can reconstruct published intelligence state.
- Ablations and disagreement sets are reproducible and attributable to run versions.
- Cutover and quarantine decisions can be justified with explicit evidence rather than intuition.
notes: This workstream closes the program loop: it proves the new substrate is useful and safe enough to publish, and it should remain blocked until the prerequisite lanes are real.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text:
Background and sequencing rationale:
- This workstream closes the loop by proving the new producer-side substrate is replay-safe, incrementally valuable, and governable enough to publish.
- Task order matters: q88.14.1 extends point-in-time replay, q88.14.2 runs ablations, q88.14.3 compares shadow output to current behavior, and q88.14.4 turns the findings into release thresholds and cutover policy.
- The workstream is intentionally blocked on the narrative, filing, structural, publishing, and observability endpoints because evaluation should operate on the real end-state lane outputs.
- Main invariants: no-lookahead replay, experiment reproducibility, disagreement-set traceability, and explicit go/no-go criteria for production cutover.

title: Extend point-in-time engine to claims, assertions, filings, lane runs, and manifests
description:
Deliverable:
- Extend the point-in-time engine to claims, assertions, filings, lane runs, and manifests.

Close criteria:
- Backtests can reconstruct the new intelligence substrate without look-ahead leakage.
design: Build on the existing backtest engine and point-in-time services so they understand bitemporal claim/assertion state and manifest publication history. Preserve no-lookahead guarantees and make sure replay chooses the correct published state for any evaluation timestamp.
acceptance_criteria:
- Point-in-time replay covers claims, assertions, filings, lane runs, and manifests.
- No-lookahead behavior is preserved.
- Later ablation tasks can run on the new substrate.
notes: If replay cannot reconstruct the published state cleanly, the rest of the evaluation story will be suspect.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: backtesting cannot validate the new architecture until it can reconstruct claims, assertions, filings, lane runs, and manifests point-in-time. Must remain true: no-lookahead behavior survives the expansion. Close evidence: replay tests rebuild published state for arbitrary evaluation timestamps.

title: Run ablations for baseline, narrative, filing, structure, and divergence
description:
Deliverable:
- Run ablations across baseline, narrative, filing, structure, and divergence configurations.

Close criteria:
- The repo can quantify which layers add value and where the new intelligence stack may be overfitting.
design: Define reproducible experiment slices that add the new intelligence layers one at a time. Preserve run-version attribution and ensure the output can be compared fairly against the baseline and intermediate variants.
acceptance_criteria:
- Ablation configurations exist for the named layers.
- Results are attributable to concrete run versions.
- Later release decisions can use the ablation outputs directly.
notes: Ablations are necessary to keep architectural enthusiasm honest.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: the program needs to know which intelligence layers add value and which only add complexity. Must remain true: experiment slices are reproducible and attributable to concrete run versions. Close evidence: ablation outputs exist for baseline, narrative, filing, structure, and divergence variants.

title: Generate shadow-vs-current disagreement sets and QA summaries
description:
Deliverable:
- Generate shadow-vs-current disagreement sets and QA summaries comparing the new intelligence outputs with the repo's current behavior.

Close criteria:
- Rollout decisions can inspect where the new system materially disagrees with the current one.
design: Compare new published outputs against the current theme/narrative behavior in a way that preserves concrete examples and run provenance. Preserve disagreement evidence so investigations can distinguish genuine improvement from regression or data/contract bugs.
acceptance_criteria:
- Disagreement sets are generated and attributable.
- QA summaries exist for shadow comparisons.
- Investigations can trace disagreements back to runs and evidence.
notes: This task should produce decision-support artifacts, not just aggregate headline metrics.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: launch decisions need concrete disagreement evidence, not only aggregate metrics. Must remain true: shadow-vs-current comparisons remain traceable back to runs and source evidence. Close evidence: disagreement sets and QA summaries explain where the new system differs materially.

title: Define publish thresholds, quarantine policy, and production cutover checklist
description:
Deliverable:
- Define publish thresholds, quarantine policy, and the production cutover checklist for the new intelligence layer.

Close criteria:
- The repo has explicit release gates and rollback/quarantine rules for moving from shadow mode to production publication.
design: Turn evaluation and observability outputs into explicit go/no-go rules. Preserve the ability to quarantine bad lanes or revert safely and avoid vague launch criteria that depend on one-off human judgment.
acceptance_criteria:
- Publish thresholds are documented in the issue and/or code-adjacent artifacts.
- Quarantine and cutover rules are explicit.
- Release readiness can be evaluated against concrete criteria.
notes: This is the final policy layer that keeps the new intelligence program from shipping on intuition alone.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: release criteria must be explicit before this program can claim success. Must remain true: publish thresholds, quarantine triggers, and cutover checks are concrete enough to drive go/no-go decisions. Close evidence: the team can evaluate readiness against documented rules rather than intuition.

title: Filing Adoption, Drift, and Divergence
description:
Why this exists:
- The plan's main correctness upgrade is to compare public narrative strength with real disclosure change rather than trusting chatter alone.

Deliverables:
- Filing adoption scores.
- Peer-normalized drift decomposition.
- Divergence alerts, reason codes, and explanation objects.
- Manifest-keyed filing lane outputs and summaries.
design: Build on normalized filing data and resolved assertions to produce explainable, decomposed signals rather than one opaque score. Touch filing-scoring modules, publish builders, migrations, and signal tests. Preserve issuer-theme lineage, section-level traceability, and peer normalization assumptions.
acceptance_criteria:
- Filing adoption, drift, and divergence outputs exist with explanation payloads.
- Outputs are publishable by lane and comparable point-in-time.
- Later consumers can tell whether narrative is corroborated, absent, or contradicted by filings.
notes: This workstream is intentionally P2 because it builds on earlier lanes, but it is strategically central to avoiding hype-only intelligence.

title: Structural Intelligence and Second-Order Baskets
description:
Why this exists:
- The repo already has a causal graph, but the merged plan requires assertion-derived structural intelligence and explanation-ready baskets rather than mostly manual graph edges.

Deliverables:
- Assertion-derived typed relations and history.
- 1-hop/2-hop path scoring with confidence, freshness, sign, and corroboration.
- Thematic baskets and second-order beneficiary outputs.
- Manifest-keyed path explanations.
design: Extend src/graph from manual causal storage toward claim/assertion-derived structure without discarding recursive CTE strengths. Touch graph schemas/storage/propagation, new basket builders, publish tables, and structural tests. Preserve explainability and keep force-graph style visualization secondary to decision-ready path explanations.
acceptance_criteria:
- Structural outputs are derived from assertions and remain explainable.
- Path scores and baskets are publishable and replayable.
- Second-order names can be surfaced with evidence-backed rationale rather than opaque graph magic.
notes: This should feel like an evolution of the current graph subsystem, not a bolt-on science project. Recursive CTEs remain the core traversal mechanism.

title: Publishing, Read Models, and Export Artifacts
description:
Why this exists:
- All upstream lanes need a single publication mechanism that turns producer tables into stable, read-only published intelligence for downstream consumers.

Deliverables:
- Read-model builders for published_* tables.
- Lane freshness and quality status handling.
- Composite manifest assembly and atomic pointer advancement.
- Immutable bundle artifacts, manifest.json, rollups.sqlite, and checksums.
design: Keep publication logic producer-owned and manifest-centric. Touch src/publish, contract registry, bundle export code, migrations, and contract/bundle parity tests. Preserve atomic pointer semantics, lane quarantine behavior, and parity between PostgreSQL and exported bundles.
acceptance_criteria:
- Published read models, lane freshness, manifests, and bundle artifacts exist.
- Pointer movement is atomic and guarded by quality status.
- Direct bundle consumption can reflect the same point-in-time state as PostgreSQL publication.
notes: If a lane produces data that cannot be published through this path, the lane is incomplete. Publication is not an afterthought; it is the boundary contract.

title: Observability, Governance, and Reliability
description:
Why this exists:
- This program adds multiple new failure modes: missing lineage, stale evidence, parser drift, contract incompatibility, and bad publishes.

Deliverables:
- Metrics for lineage completeness, unresolved entities, filing parse quality, and stale evidence.
- Per-lane tracing, failure-rate dashboards, and freshness budgets.
- Publish/bundle/coverage drift metrics and contract enforcement.
- Replay, quarantine, and operator inspection hooks.
design: Extend existing observability foundations rather than inventing a separate ops surface. Touch src/observability, metrics/tracing integration in each new subsystem, QA checks, and reliability tests. Preserve the invariant that publish and replay safety are observable first-class concerns, not post-launch cleanups.
acceptance_criteria:
- Critical intelligence quality, freshness, and publish metrics exist.
- Failures can be detected, quarantined, and inspected without guesswork.
- New lanes expose the telemetry needed for reliable shadow and production operation.
notes: This workstream starts early but finishes late. Keep it open until the new claims, filings, assertions, and publish machinery all emit meaningful telemetry.

title: Backtesting, Shadow Evaluation, and Cutover
description:
Why this exists:
- The program is only worth shipping if the new intelligence layer is replay-safe, quantitatively useful, and governable at release time.

Deliverables:
- Point-in-time replay extensions for claims, assertions, filings, lane runs, and manifests.
- Ablation runs across narrative, filing, structure, and divergence layers.
- Shadow-vs-current disagreement sets and QA summaries.
- Publish thresholds, quarantine policy, and cutover checklist.
design: Extend src/backtest and evaluation tooling to operate on the new producer-side substrate rather than old theme-only state. Touch replay services, audit/versioning code, metrics, QA reporting, and release criteria. Preserve no-lookahead behavior, reproducibility, and explicit publish gates before any cutover decisions.
acceptance_criteria:
- Point-in-time replays can reconstruct published intelligence state.
- Ablations and disagreement sets are reproducible and attributable to run versions.
- Cutover and quarantine decisions can be justified with explicit evidence rather than intuition.
notes: This workstream closes the program loop: it proves the new substrate is useful and safe enough to publish, and it should remain blocked until the prerequisite lanes are real.

title: Canonical Identity and Coverage
description:
Why this exists:
- Claims, filings, graph edges, and narrative frames are only useful if the repo has durable canonical IDs and explicit coverage maturity.

Deliverables:
- Concept registry, aliases, issuer/security crosswalks, and higher-order entity mappings.
- Coverage profiles, tier history, pack membership, and semiconductors Pack 1 seed assets.

Exclusions:
- No parallel identity model outside news-tracker.
design: Extend the current security-master foundation into a broader concept system without breaking existing ticker-centric flows. Touch src/security_master, new src/coverage modules, migrations, seed/evaluation fixtures, and read-model tests. Preserve the invariant that theme concepts and narrative frames are distinct objects and that coverage is explicit rather than inferred.
acceptance_criteria:
- Canonical concept and coverage tables exist with clear ownership.
- Symbols, themes, and packs can resolve to durable IDs and explicit tiers.
- Later claims, narrative, filing, and graph work can depend on one shared identity layer.
notes: This is the repo's semantic backbone. Future shortcuts that bypass canonical IDs or hide weak coverage should be treated as regressions.
close_reason: All 4 children complete. Concept registry (concepts, aliases, issuer-security crosswalk), structural relationships (13 edge types), theme/narrative links (4 link types), coverage profiles (4 tiers with bitemporal history), domain packs, and semiconductors Pack 1 seed (23 issuers, 7 technologies, 6 themes, 18 supply chain edges). Migrations 019-021, 3 new modules (concept_schemas, concept_repository, coverage). All acceptance criteria met.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text:
Background and sequencing rationale:
- The current repo already has a security master, but the merged plan needs a broader semantic identity layer that can represent issuers, securities, facilities, products, technologies, theme concepts, narrative frames, and explicit coverage maturity.
- Task order matters: q88.2.1 broadens the security master into canonical identity, q88.2.2 adds the higher-order mappings later lanes need, q88.2.3 makes coverage explicit over time, and q88.2.4 turns semiconductors into the reference Pack 1 implementation.
- This workstream is also the honesty mechanism for the whole program: it prevents the system from pretending every sector has equal structural maturity on day one.
- Main invariants: theme concepts stay distinct from narrative frames, canonical IDs stay producer-owned, and weak coverage is shown explicitly instead of hidden.

title: Extend security master into concept registry, alias model, and issuer/security crosswalk
description:
Deliverable:
- Extend the current security-master foundation into a broader concept registry with aliases, issuer/security crosswalks, and canonical concept IDs.

Close criteria:
- Claims, narratives, filings, and graph tasks have one durable identity anchor to target.
design: Build forward from src/security_master instead of creating a competing identity subsystem. Include canonical IDs, alias normalization, issuer/security relationships, and enough migration scaffolding to coexist with current ticker-centric lookups. Preserve replay safety and deterministic resolution behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- Concept registry and alias/crosswalk tables exist.
- Current security master can resolve into canonical concept IDs.
- Downstream lanes can depend on one shared identity substrate.
notes: This task should feel like a careful extension of existing entity infrastructure, not a throwaway rewrite.
close_reason: Concept registry extends security master with canonical concept IDs, alias model, and issuer/security crosswalk. Migration 019 creates concepts, concept_aliases, issuer_security_map tables and adds optional concept_id to securities. ConceptRepository with upsert, fuzzy search, alias resolution, and crosswalk queries. Deterministic make_concept_id() for replay safety. 28 tests pass.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: the current security master is too narrow for claim-first intelligence. Must remain true: canonical IDs stay deterministic and producer-owned. Close evidence: concept registry, alias model, and issuer/security crosswalks resolve current repo entities consistently.

title: Add subsidiary, facility, supplier-customer, product, technology, and theme/narrative mappings
description:
Deliverable:
- Add the additional concept mappings needed by the merged plan: subsidiaries, facilities, supplier-customer entities, products, technologies, and explicit theme/narrative links.

Close criteria:
- The identity layer can represent the higher-order entities later claims and graph work need.
design: Separate theme concepts from narrative frames and model the additional entity families in a way that supports later relation extraction without collapsing everything into a generic blob. Preserve canonical IDs and explicit relationship tables rather than encoding structure only in JSON.
acceptance_criteria:
- Additional entity families and mapping tables exist.
- Theme concepts and narrative frames are modeled distinctly.
- Later filing, claim, and graph tasks can reference these mappings directly.
notes: This task exists to prevent later shortcuts where complex entities are stuffed into free-form fields and become unresolvable.
close_reason: Migration 020 adds concept_relationships (13 structural edge types with composite PK, confidence, attribution) and concept_theme_links (4 link types: covers/driven_by/impacts/monitors). ConceptRelationship and ConceptThemeLink dataclasses with validation. ConceptRepository extended with upsert_relationship, get_relationships (directional), upsert_theme_link, get_theme_links, get_themes_for_concept. 83 tests pass across the security master module.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: later claims/graph/filing tasks need more than issuers and tickers. Must remain true: theme concepts and narrative frames remain distinct, and higher-order entities are modeled explicitly. Close evidence: these mapping tables exist and later lanes can reference them directly.

title: Add coverage profiles, coverage tier history, and pack membership tables
description:
Deliverable:
- Add coverage profiles, coverage tier history, domain-pack membership, and explicit maturity publication primitives.

Close criteria:
- The repo can state what it covers well, what it covers partially, and what remains unsupported.
design: Make coverage explicit by symbol, theme, and pack rather than implied. Include history so maturity can evolve over time without rewriting the past. Preserve the invariant that weak coverage degrades gracefully instead of masquerading as full structural intelligence.
acceptance_criteria:
- Coverage profiles and history tables exist.
- Pack membership and tier semantics are explicit and versionable.
- Later publication work can surface coverage without inference hacks.
notes: Coverage honesty is part of the product quality bar; this is not cosmetic metadata.
close_reason: Migration 021 creates coverage_profiles (tier + completeness signals), coverage_tier_history (bitemporal append-only log), domain_packs (named coverage domains), and domain_pack_members (concept membership with anchor/member/peripheral roles). Python dataclasses with validation. 26 tests pass.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: coverage honesty must be built into the substrate, not layered on later. Must remain true: maturity is explicit by symbol/theme/pack and can change over time without rewriting history. Close evidence: coverage profiles and tier history can be queried and published.

title: Seed semiconductors Pack 1 dictionaries, priors, and evaluation fixtures
description:
Deliverable:
- Seed semiconductors as Pack 1 with dictionaries, priors, overrides, and evaluation fixtures that encode the current highest-confidence domain knowledge.

Close criteria:
- The new identity/coverage system has one real domain pack that downstream tasks can exercise end-to-end.
design: Start with semiconductors because the repo's current knowledge base is strongest there. Package lexicons, priors, overrides, and evaluation examples so later sector expansion follows a concrete template instead of folklore. Preserve repeatable seeding and testability.
acceptance_criteria:
- Semiconductors Pack 1 seed assets exist.
- Pack fixtures can drive tests and evaluation workflows.
- Later pack expansion has a concrete reference implementation.
notes: Future self should treat this as the gold reference pack, not a one-off experiment hidden in ad hoc config.
close_reason: Semiconductors Pack 1 seed with 23 issuers, 7 technologies, 6 themes, 18 supply chain edges. Pure build_seed_objects() produces 50+ concepts, 80+ aliases, coverage profiles per concept, and pack membership with roles. JSON seed file at src/coverage/data/semiconductors_pack_1.json serves as gold reference template. 49 tests verify content integrity, referential consistency, and template reusability.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: the new pack architecture needs one real reference implementation. Must remain true: semiconductors Pack 1 is seedable, testable, and reusable as the template for expansion. Close evidence: dictionaries/priors/fixtures exist and drive evaluation workflows.

title: SEC Filing Lane
description:
Why this exists:
- The merged plan depends on structured SEC ingestion, stable section IDs, XBRL support, and reliable lineage rather than fragile filing scraping.

Deliverables:
- FilingProvider interface.
- EdgarToolsProvider primary path and SecApiProvider fallback.
- Filing artifact, section, attachment, XBRL, and diff persistence.
- Parser regression corpus and sequential alignment support.

Exclusions:
- No filing parsing logic in stock-screener.
design: Build filing ingestion as an intelligence-plane subsystem under src/filings. Centralize SEC identity/rate-limit policy and persist hashes, timestamps, and run lineage for replay. Touch new provider modules, migrations, parsing/diff workers, and regression tests. Primary risks are parser brittleness, inconsistent lineage, and fallback drift between providers.
acceptance_criteria:
- Primary and fallback filing providers exist behind one interface.
- Filings, sections, XBRL facts, and diffs persist with stable lineage.
- Downstream adoption/drift work has normalized filing data to consume.
notes: The goal is not just to fetch filings, but to make them replayable, comparable, and usable as evidence inputs for later scoring and divergence work.
close_reason: All 5 children complete. SEC Filing Lane delivers: FilingProvider ABC with centralized SEC policy, EdgarToolsProvider (primary, edgartools-backed), SecApiProvider (fallback, direct EDGAR HTTP), filing persistence (4 tables: filings, sections, attachments, XBRL facts with bitemporal timestamps and hashes), section alignment/diff logic, and parser regression corpus (8 hard cases). 148 filing tests total.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text:
Background and sequencing rationale:
- Filing intelligence is only credible if the repo can ingest filings in a structured, replayable way with section-level and artifact-level lineage. This workstream is the answer to that requirement.
- Task order matters: q88.3.1 defines the common provider contract and SEC policy, q88.3.2 and q88.3.3 implement the primary and fallback providers, q88.3.4 persists artifacts/sections/XBRL with lineage, and q88.3.5 builds the section-alignment/diff capability needed for drift work.
- The real goal is not “fetch filings”; it is “make filings usable as evidence.”
- Main invariants: edgartools is the primary path, the direct SEC API is the fallback/control plane, and both paths must emit compatible lineage.

title: Define FilingProvider interface, SEC identity policy, and rate-limit policy
description:
Deliverable:
- Define the FilingProvider interface plus central SEC identity and rate-limit policy for the new filing lane.

Close criteria:
- Primary and fallback providers can share one policy and one producer-owned abstraction.
design: Abstract filing access before implementation details harden. Include provider responsibilities, retry/rate-limit expectations, and the lineage fields every provider must emit. Preserve a single policy surface so edgartools and direct SEC fallback do not drift.
acceptance_criteria:
- FilingProvider interface exists.
- SEC identity and rate-limit policy are centralized.
- Provider implementations have one contract to satisfy.
notes: This task is about boundary discipline; later providers should not invent their own output shapes or compliance behavior.
close_reason: Filing lane module at src/filing/ with FilingProvider ABC (fetch_filing, search_filings), SECPolicy (centralized User-Agent, rate limits, URLs, headers), SECRateLimiter (per-second sliding window), FilingConfig, and output schemas (FilingIdentity, FilingResult, FilingSection). 34 tests verify provider contract, SEC policy defaults, schema validation, and filing type coverage.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: both filing providers need one contract and one SEC policy surface. Must remain true: providers emit the same lineage-critical fields and respect centralized SEC identity/rate-limit rules. Close evidence: FilingProvider plus policy definitions exist and both implementations can target them.

title: Implement EdgarToolsProvider primary ingestion path
description:
Deliverable:
- Implement EdgarToolsProvider as the primary filing ingestion and parsing path.

Close criteria:
- news-tracker can fetch, normalize, and expose filing content through the primary provider contract.
design: Build the main provider around edgartools because it gives the richest typed access to filings, sections, and XBRL-related structures. Preserve stable artifact identifiers, timestamps, and provider-neutral output so fallback logic can interoperate cleanly.
acceptance_criteria:
- EdgarToolsProvider exists behind FilingProvider.
- Primary provider emits normalized filing metadata and artifacts.
- Provider behavior is covered by regression tests.
notes: This is the primary path, so it sets the quality bar for everything downstream.
close_reason: EdgarToolsProvider implemented with lazy import, sync-to-async bridging via thread executor, section extraction (structured + full-text fallback), filing type normalization (12 types + amendments + aliases), deterministic section IDs, and error handling (failed results instead of exceptions). 28 new tests cover pure functions (normalization, date parsing, section extraction) and mocked provider operations (fetch, search, not-found, errors). edgartools added to pyproject.toml dependencies.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: edgartools is the primary structured filing path in the merged plan. Must remain true: provider output is normalized and provider-neutral enough for fallback parity. Close evidence: primary ingestion tests cover representative forms and artifact extraction.

title: Implement SecApiProvider fallback and reconciliation path
description:
Deliverable:
- Implement the direct SEC API fallback/reconciliation provider used when the primary path fails or needs low-level validation.

Close criteria:
- The filing lane has a fallback path that preserves the same lineage guarantees as the primary provider.
design: Keep the fallback narrow: inventory retrieval, reconciliation, control-plane validation, and recovery when primary parsing fails. Preserve the same filing/provider contract and hashes so the rest of the system never needs provider-specific branching.
acceptance_criteria:
- SecApiProvider exists and satisfies FilingProvider.
- Fallback/reconciliation flows preserve lineage compatibility.
- Tests cover primary/fallback parity expectations.
notes: The fallback exists to improve reliability, not to fork the pipeline into two competing implementations.
close_reason: SecApiProvider implements FilingProvider using direct SEC EDGAR HTTP APIs (EFTS search + archive download). Narrow fallback: inventory retrieval via EFTS full-text search, filing text recovery from archives, same lineage output as EdgarToolsProvider. 26 new tests cover fetch (success/not-found/error), search (success/empty/error/limit), and provider parity (same output shape, deterministic section IDs).

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: reliability requires a fallback/control-plane path before filing intelligence becomes a dependency. Must remain true: fallback does not fork the data model. Close evidence: reconciliation tests show compatibility with the primary provider's lineage and hashes.

title: Persist raw filings, sections, attachments, XBRL facts, hashes, and lineage
description:
Deliverable:
- Persist raw filings, normalized sections, attachments, XBRL facts, hashes, and lineage/timestamp metadata needed for replay.

Close criteria:
- The database can hold filing artifacts in a way that supports later claim extraction, drift analysis, and auditability.
design: Model filing persistence at the artifact and section level. Include source_published_at, ingested_at, stable section identifiers, hashes, and run lineage. Preserve traceability from every downstream score or claim back to a concrete filing artifact.
acceptance_criteria:
- Filing artifact and section tables persist normalized data.
- XBRL facts and hashes are stored with lineage fields.
- Later extraction and drift tasks can query filing state point-in-time.
notes: If downstream work cannot trace back to concrete filing artifacts, this task is incomplete.
close_reason: Migration 022 creates filings (keyed by accession_number with bitemporal timestamps, content hash, concept FK), filing_sections (deterministic IDs, section-level hashes), filing_attachments, and filing_xbrl_facts tables. FilingRepository with upsert/query for filings, sections, and XBRL facts. filing_result_to_records() converts provider output to persistence records with computed hashes. 25 new tests.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: filing-derived intelligence is impossible without artifact- and section-level persistence. Must remain true: every later claim/score can trace back to concrete filing artifacts. Close evidence: tables hold sections, attachments, XBRL facts, hashes, and timestamps with replay-safe lineage.

title: Build sequential section alignment/diff logic and parser regression corpus
description:
Deliverable:
- Build sequential filing section alignment/diff logic and a parser regression corpus for tricky issuer/form combinations.

Close criteria:
- Later drift and divergence tasks can compare filings reliably instead of using brittle whole-document diffs.
design: Treat section alignment as a first-class capability, not an afterthought. Compare equivalent sections across sequential filings, capture diff lineage, and keep a regression corpus for issuers/forms that historically break parsing assumptions. Preserve reproducibility and audit trails for diff outputs.
acceptance_criteria:
- Section alignment/diff logic exists.
- Parser regression corpus covers hard cases.
- Filing comparison outputs are replayable and attributable to source filings.
notes: This task is the bridge between raw filing ETL and the later decomposed drift-scoring work.
close_reason: Section alignment/diff logic (SectionAligner with fuzzy name matching via SequenceMatcher, SectionDiff with line-level stats, compare_filings() entry point) and parser regression corpus (8 cases covering 10-K, 20-F, 6-K, 8-K, SC 13D with known parsing challenges). 35 tests cover normalization, alignment (exact/fuzzy/added/removed), diffs (identical/changed/removed), full comparison, and corpus validation.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: drift and divergence require aligned section-level comparison, not brittle whole-document diffs. Must remain true: diff outputs remain attributable to source filings and diff runs. Close evidence: alignment logic and a parser regression corpus cover hard issuer/form cases.

title: Evidence Claims and Resolver Cascade
description:
Why this exists:
- The architecture pivots from theme-centric heuristics to evidence claims resolved through conservative entity and relation handling.

Deliverables:
- evidence_claims schema with idempotent keys and lineage rules.
- Deterministic resolver cascade over security master, aliases, and context.
- Controlled LLM fallback.
- Review triggers, reviewer queue backend, and claim governance hooks.

Exclusions:
- No silent promotion of low-confidence, high-impact claims to authoritative state.
design: Layer deterministic resolution ahead of expensive or ambiguous fallbacks. Touch new src/claims modules, security master extensions, review state plumbing, migrations, and quality/dead-letter tests. Preserve the invariant that high-impact edges are reviewable and that unresolved ambiguity becomes queued work rather than hidden data corruption.
acceptance_criteria:
- Claim creation is idempotent, lineaged, and review-aware.
- Resolver tiers are explicit and testable.
- Ambiguous or risky cases route into review/quarantine paths instead of directly mutating authoritative intelligence.
notes: This workstream creates the atomic substrate for everything downstream. If a later task needs truth without claim provenance, the missing claim should be fixed here, not papered over elsewhere.
close_reason: All 5 subtasks complete: evidence_claims schema (q88.4.1), deterministic resolver cascade (q88.4.2), LLM fallback gate (q88.4.3), review queue with triggers (q88.4.4), quality checks and dead-letter handling (q88.4.5). Claims are idempotent, resolver tiers are explicit, ambiguous/risky cases route to review/quarantine.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text:
Background and sequencing rationale:
- This workstream creates the atomic evidence substrate that replaces theme-centric heuristics with claim-centric intelligence.
- Task order matters: q88.4.1 defines the claim record itself, q88.4.2 establishes the deterministic resolver core, q88.4.3 constrains model fallback, q88.4.4 creates the review backend state for ambiguity, and q88.4.5 adds safe failure/quarantine behavior.
- The reason this is conservative is architectural, not stylistic: high-impact claims that cannot be grounded safely should become explicit review work instead of silent authoritative state.
- Main invariants: claims are idempotent, lineage-rich, review-aware, and never rely on LLM output as an unreviewed source of canonical truth.

title: Define evidence_claims schema, idempotent claim keys, and claim lineage rules
description:
Deliverable:
- Define the evidence_claims schema, idempotent claim keys, and the baseline lineage/temporal fields for atomic claim storage.

Close criteria:
- The repo has one authoritative claim record shape that downstream assertion and scoring work can trust.
design: Make claims atomic, auditable, and replayable. Include source references, spans, subject/predicate/object structure, confidence metadata, and bitemporal fields. Preserve deterministic idempotent keys so retries or replays do not silently duplicate evidence.
acceptance_criteria:
- evidence_claims schema exists with lineage and idempotent-key rules.
- Claim writes can be retried safely.
- Later assertion work can consume a stable atomic substrate.
notes: If a future feature wants evidence without a claim row, it is likely bypassing the intended architecture.
close_reason: Migration 023 creates news_intel.evidence_claims with deterministic claim_key (UNIQUE), subject/predicate/object structure, concept FK resolution, source spans, bitemporal validity, extraction method, and contract versioning. ClaimRepository with idempotent upsert (ON CONFLICT claim_key), concept resolution updates, filtered listing. make_claim_key() is case-insensitive and whitespace-trimmed for replay safety. 32 tests pass.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: the claim row is the atomic intelligence unit the rest of the plan assumes. Must remain true: claim writes are idempotent, lineaged, and expressive enough to model source spans plus subject/predicate/object structure. Close evidence: retries do not duplicate claims and downstream assertion code can consume this schema directly.

title: Implement deterministic resolver tiers using security master, aliases, and local context
description:
Deliverable:
- Implement the deterministic resolver cascade over security master, aliases, and local context for entity/relation grounding.

Close criteria:
- The system resolves the common cases cheaply and consistently before any fuzzy or model-heavy fallback path is used.
design: Order the cascade explicitly: security master/ticker/CIK, alias/dictionary rules, then local-context resolution. Keep confidence decomposition visible. Preserve deterministic resolution for routine cases and make it easy to observe where ambiguous passages enter later review or fallback paths.
acceptance_criteria:
- Resolver tiers are implemented and testable.
- Common deterministic cases resolve without LLM fallback.
- Confidence decomposition is available for later review logic.
notes: This task exists to keep the claim layer stable and affordable; do not let fallback complexity swallow the deterministic core.
close_reason: EntityResolver implements 3-tier deterministic cascade: Tier 1 (exact ticker/CIK/concept_id, confidence 1.0), Tier 2 (case-insensitive alias dictionary, confidence 0.95), Tier 3 (fuzzy pg_trgm similarity, confidence 0.6-0.7). ResolverResult carries concept, tier, confidence, alternatives, and metadata for audit. resolve_batch() for bulk resolution. 21 new tests cover each tier independently and the full cascade (empty/whitespace, batch, confidence ordering, type filtering).

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: cheap deterministic grounding should do the bulk of the work before ambiguity reaches review or models. Must remain true: routine resolutions are reproducible and confidence decomposition stays visible. Close evidence: tiered resolver tests cover security-master, alias, and local-context cases without LLM fallback.

title: Gate LLM fallback to unresolved/high-value passages only
description:
Deliverable:
- Constrain LLM usage to unresolved or high-value passages and formalize that gating in the resolver pipeline.

Close criteria:
- Model fallback is a controlled exception path rather than the default path for claim creation.
design: Define the gating rules, confidence thresholds, and high-value passage criteria that justify invoking an LLM. Preserve full provenance about when and why fallback was used, and ensure LLM proposals do not directly create unreviewed authoritative entities or high-impact relations.
acceptance_criteria:
- LLM fallback is explicitly gated.
- Fallback invocations are provenance-rich and auditable.
- High-impact claims still require deterministic grounding or review before becoming authoritative.
notes: The intent is disciplined escalation, not banning models entirely. Make the guardrails visible and enforceable.
close_reason: LLM fallback gate implemented: ClaimsConfig (master switch, passage length, predicate filter, budget caps), FallbackGate (pure policy engine), FallbackProvenance (audit records), FallbackBudget (daily+per-run tracking), LLM_PROPOSED resolver tier, 31 gate tests + 5 integration tests, all 84 claims tests pass

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: fallback model usage needs explicit discipline before the claim layer hardens. Must remain true: model calls are auditable exception paths and do not directly create unreviewed canonical truth. Close evidence: gating rules, provenance, and tests show when fallback is allowed and what happens next.

title: Add review triggers, reviewer queue backend, and merge/split proposal plumbing
description:
Deliverable:
- Add review triggers, reviewer queue backend state, and merge/split proposal plumbing for ambiguous or high-impact claim outcomes.

Close criteria:
- Ambiguity produces queued review work instead of silently mutating canonical truth.
design: Model review as authoritative backend state owned by news-tracker. Capture trigger reasons such as low-margin matches, contradiction detections, and risky supplier/customer claims. Preserve recompute-friendly lineage so later decisions can flow back into assertions cleanly.
acceptance_criteria:
- Review triggers and queue backend exist.
- Merge/split proposals are modeled explicitly.
- Later review decisions can be linked back to concrete claims and recompute paths.
notes: This is not UI work. The goal is to create trustworthy backend review state for downstream consumers to act on.
close_reason: Review queue implemented: ReviewTask schema with 4 task types and state machine, 4 stateless trigger functions (low_confidence, llm_proposed, contradiction, high_impact_predicate), merge/split proposal builders, ReviewRepository with idempotent upserts, migration 024 with GIN indexes for array lookups, 54 tests, all 138 claims tests pass

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: ambiguous or high-impact claims must become managed review work instead of silent state mutation. Must remain true: reviewer queue state, merge/split proposals, and trigger reasons remain backend-owned and linked to concrete claims. Close evidence: review tasks can be generated, stored, and later fed into recompute paths.

title: Add claim quality checks, dead-letter handling, and quarantine rules
description:
Deliverable:
- Add claim quality checks, dead-letter handling, and quarantine rules for unrecoverable extraction failures or unsafe outputs.

Close criteria:
- The claim lane can fail safely without silently polluting authoritative data.
design: Make failure handling explicit: quality thresholds, dead-letter capture, quarantine criteria, and the observability hooks needed to detect them. Preserve enough context for replay or manual inspection and avoid dropping problematic evidence without a trace.
acceptance_criteria:
- Claim quality checks and failure states exist.
- Dead-letter/quarantine paths preserve context.
- The lane can be monitored and replayed after failure analysis.
notes: A robust claim layer is as much about safe failure as successful extraction.
close_reason: Quality checks, dead-letter handling, and quarantine rules implemented: run_quality_checks with 3-tier verdict (accept/quarantine/dead_letter), 9 check codes, DeadLetterRecord with deterministic IDs, capture_dead_letter and verdict_to_dead_letter builders, DeadLetterRepository, migration 025, ClaimsConfig quality settings, 42 tests, all 180 claims tests pass

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text: Why now: extraction systems need a safe failure path before they are trusted with authoritative state. Must remain true: bad or unsafe claim outputs are quarantined or dead-lettered with full context, not dropped or published. Close evidence: failure-state tests preserve replay/debug information.

title: Assertion Resolution and Derived Edge Models
description:
Why this exists:
- Raw claims are too granular and unstable to act as the serving substrate for graph, scoring, or published outputs.

Deliverables:
- resolved_assertions, support links, contradiction links, and validity windows.
- Aggregation logic over support, freshness, diversity, and review state.
- Assertion-derived edge/history/exposure/path-cache inputs.
- Recompute hooks after review decisions.
design: Keep assertions as the stable current-belief layer between claims and every downstream lane. Touch new src/assertions modules, graph adapters, migrations, and assertion/edge recompute tests. Preserve the invariant that derived edges and scores consume assertions rather than raw claims directly, and that contradictions remain explainable rather than discarded.
acceptance_criteria:
- Assertion tables and recompute paths exist.
- Current belief, support, contradiction, and freshness can be queried and explained.
- Downstream graph, scoring, and publication work can depend on assertions as the authoritative intermediate layer.
notes: Treat this as the seam that turns extraction output into publishable intelligence. If graph or scoring logic wants to skip it, the design has drifted.
close_reason: All 4 subtasks complete: assertion schemas+links+validity (q88.5.1), aggregation with explainable confidence (q88.5.2), edge derivation+exposures+path-cache (q88.5.3), review-triggered recompute with audit trail (q88.5.4). Downstream consumers read assertions and derived edges, not raw claims.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text:
Background and sequencing rationale:
- Assertions are the seam that turns raw evidence into stable current belief. Without this layer, graph and scoring logic either overfit to raw claims or quietly reimplement assertion logic in multiple places.
- Task order matters: q88.5.1 defines the assertion tables and linkage, q88.5.2 computes stable aggregated state, q88.5.3 derives graph/exposure inputs, and q88.5.4 closes the loop by recomputing after review decisions.
- This workstream is where the claim-first architecture becomes operational for downstream consumers.
- Main invariants: derived edges and scores come from assertions, contradictions remain inspectable, and review changes flow back into authoritative state.

title: Define resolved_assertions, support links, contradiction links, and validity windows
description:
Deliverable:
- Define resolved_assertions, support-link, contradiction-link, and validity-window storage that turns atomic claims into stable current-belief objects.

Close criteria:
- The repo has a durable assertion model that downstream graph and scoring work can consume.
design: Model assertions as the aggregation seam between raw evidence and every later lane. Include support/contradiction linkage, validity intervals, and enough lineage to trace each assertion back to its constituent claims. Preserve auditable contradiction handling rather than collapsing disagreement into one silent score.
acceptance_criteria:
- Assertion tables and linkage models exist.
- Validity windows are explicit.
- Later lanes can consume assertions without reading raw claims directly.
notes: This task defines the stable belief substrate; downstream logic should not invent a second one.

title: Narrative Lane V2

title: Filing Adoption, Drift, and Divergence
description:
Why this exists:
- The plan's main correctness upgrade is to compare public narrative strength with real disclosure change rather than trusting chatter alone.

- Peer-normalized drift decomposition.
- Divergence alerts, reason codes, and explanation objects.
- Manifest-keyed filing lane outputs and summaries.

Touch filing-scoring modules, publish builders, migrations, and signal tests. Preserve issuer-theme lineage, section-level traceability, and peer normalization assumptions.

- Filing adoption, drift, and divergence outputs exist with explanation payloads.
- Outputs are publishable by lane and comparable point-in-time.

- Main invariants: issuer-theme lineage is preserved, section-level explanations remain available, and divergence outputs are interpretable enough to challenge a crowded story.

title: Structural Intelligence and Second-Order Baskets
description:
Why this exists:
- The repo already has a causal graph, but the merged plan requires assertion-derived structural intelligence and explanation-ready baskets rather than mostly manual graph edges.

Deliverables:
- Assertion-derived typed relations and history.
- 1-hop/2-hop path scoring with confidence, freshness, sign, and corroboration.
- Thematic baskets and second-order beneficiary outputs.
- Manifest-keyed path explanations.
design: Extend src/graph from manual causal storage toward claim/assertion-derived structure without discarding recursive CTE strengths. Touch graph schemas/storage/propagation, new basket builders, publish tables, and structural tests. Preserve explainability and keep force-graph style visualization secondary to decision-ready path explanations.
acceptance_criteria:
- Structural outputs are derived from assertions and remain explainable.
- Path scores and baskets are publishable and replayable.
- Second-order names can be surfaced with evidence-backed rationale rather than opaque graph magic.
notes: This should feel like an evolution of the current graph subsystem, not a bolt-on science project. Recursive CTEs remain the core traversal mechanism.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text:
Background and sequencing rationale:
- The repo already has a causal graph, but the merged plan requires it to become evidence-backed structural intelligence rather than mainly manual relationship storage.
- Task order matters: q88.8.1 converts assertions into typed structural relations, q88.8.2 scores explanation-ready paths, q88.8.3 builds baskets/beneficiaries, and q88.8.4 publishes cached path explanations.
- The key product principle here is explanation-first: the output is not a force-directed graph, it is an intelligible path describing why a second-order name surfaced.
- Main invariants: recursive CTE traversal remains the backbone, no graph database is introduced, and surfaced baskets remain traceable to evidence-backed paths.

title: Expand graph model from manual causal edges to assertion-derived typed relations

title: Publishing, Read Models, and Export Artifacts
description:
Why this exists:
- All upstream lanes need a single publication mechanism that turns producer tables into stable, read-only published intelligence for downstream consumers.

Deliverables:
- Read-model builders for published_* tables.
- Lane freshness and quality status handling.
- Composite manifest assembly and atomic pointer advancement.
- Immutable bundle artifacts, manifest.json, rollups.sqlite, and checksums.
design: Keep publication logic producer-owned and manifest-centric. Touch src/publish, contract registry, bundle export code, migrations, and contract/bundle parity tests. Preserve atomic pointer semantics, lane quarantine behavior, and parity between PostgreSQL and exported bundles.
acceptance_criteria:
- Published read models, lane freshness, manifests, and bundle artifacts exist.
- Pointer movement is atomic and guarded by quality status.
- Direct bundle consumption can reflect the same point-in-time state as PostgreSQL publication.
notes: If a lane produces data that cannot be published through this path, the lane is incomplete. Publication is not an afterthought; it is the boundary contract.

comments.author: David Ten
comments.text:
Background and sequencing rationale:
- Publication is the real serving boundary of the producer. This workstream exists so lanes do not leak unfinished data directly to consumers.

- This workstream starts after foundation, but its later tasks are intentionally blocked on the lane outputs they publish.
- Main invariants: published rows are manifest-keyed, pointer advancement is atomic, and PostgreSQL publication matches bundle publication semantically.

title: Feature 6.1: Point-in-Time Data Infrastructure
description:
Feature 6.1: Point-in-Time Data Infrastructure

PART OF: Epic 6 - Point-in-Time Backtesting

PURPOSE:
Enable retrieving data as it existed at any historical point, preventing look-ahead bias in backtests.

CRITICAL REQUIREMENT:
All backtests MUST only use data available at time T. This means:
- Use ingestion_timestamp, NOT publication_timestamp (for delayed data)
- No retrospective theme merging
- Pin model versions for reproducibility

INFRASTRUCTURE COMPONENTS:

1. Point-in-Time Theme Access:
async def get_themes_point_in_time(
    as_of: datetime,
    db: Database
) -> List[Theme]:
    '''Get themes as they existed at a specific time.'''
    return await db.fetch('''
        SELECT * FROM themes 
        WHERE created_at <= 
        AND (deleted_at IS NULL OR deleted_at > )
    ''', as_of)

2. Point-in-Time Metrics:
async def get_metrics_point_in_time(
    theme_id: str,
    as_of: datetime,
    lookback_days: int = 7
) -> List[ThemeMetrics]:
    '''Get metrics history up to a point in time.'''
    start = as_of - timedelta(days=lookback_days)
    return await db.fetch('''
        SELECT * FROM theme_metrics
        WHERE theme_id = 
        AND date >=  AND date <= 
    ''', theme_id, start.date(), as_of.date())

3. Model Version Pinning:
class ModelVersion(BaseModel):
    version_id: str
    clustering_model_path: str
    embedding_model_name: str
    created_at: datetime
    config_snapshot: Dict

def load_model_version(version_id: str) -> ClusteringService:
    '''Load specific model version for reproducible backtests.'''
    version = await db.get_model_version(version_id)
    return ClusteringService.from_checkpoint(version.clustering_model_path)

4. Price Data Integration:
class PriceDataFeed:
    '''Fetch historical price data for backtest returns.'''
    
    async def get_ohlcv(
        self,
        ticker: str,
        start_date: date,
        end_date: date
    ) -> pd.DataFrame:
        '''Get OHLCV data for ticker.'''
        # Use yfinance for MVP, paid feed for production
        pass
    
    async def get_forward_returns(
        self,
        tickers: List[str],
        as_of: date,
        horizon: int = 5
    ) -> Dict[str, float]:
        '''Get N-day forward returns from as_of date.'''
        returns = {}
        for ticker in tickers:
            prices = await self.get_ohlcv(ticker, as_of, as_of + timedelta(days=horizon+5))
            if len(prices) >= horizon:
                returns[ticker] = (prices.iloc[horizon]['close'] - prices.iloc[0]['close']) / prices.iloc[0]['close']
        return returns

5. Audit Trail:
Every backtest run records:
- Parameters used
- Model versions
- Date range
- Results
- Can be reproduced

DATABASE ADDITIONS:

- backtest_runs table

title: Feature 1.1: Database Schema for Themes
description:
Feature 1.1: Database Schema for Themes

PART OF: Epic 1 - Theme Clustering Foundation

PURPOSE:
Create the PostgreSQL schema to store themes and their time-series metrics. This is the data foundation - without it, clustering results have nowhere to persist.

WHY POSTGRESQL (NOT SEPARATE DB):
- Already have pgvector for embeddings
- Themes need to JOIN with documents table
- Transactional consistency for theme updates
- Simpler ops than adding another database

TABLES TO CREATE:

1. themes table:
   - theme_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY (format: 'theme_{hash}')
   - name TEXT NOT NULL (auto-generated from top keywords)
   - description TEXT (optional longer description)
   - centroid vector(768) NOT NULL (mean embedding of cluster)
   - top_keywords TEXT[] DEFAULT '{}' (c-TF-IDF keywords)
   - top_tickers TEXT[] DEFAULT '{}' (most mentioned tickers)
   - top_entities JSONB DEFAULT '{}' (companies, products, technologies)
   - document_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0 (cached count)
   - lifecycle_stage TEXT DEFAULT 'emerging' (emerging/accelerating/mature/fading)
   - created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
   - updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
   - metadata JSONB DEFAULT '{}' (extensible)

2. theme_metrics table (time series):
   - theme_id TEXT REFERENCES themes(theme_id) ON DELETE CASCADE
   - date DATE NOT NULL
   - document_count INTEGER
   - sentiment_score REAL (-1 to 1)
   - volume_zscore REAL
   - velocity REAL
   - acceleration REAL
   - avg_authority REAL
   - PRIMARY KEY (theme_id, date)

INDICES:
- themes: HNSW on centroid for vector similarity
- themes: GIN on top_keywords for array contains
- themes: B-tree on lifecycle_stage
- theme_metrics: B-tree on date for time range queries

MIGRATION CONSIDERATIONS:
- Use IF NOT EXISTS for idempotent migrations
- Add foreign key from documents.theme_ids to themes (or keep loose coupling)
- Consider partitioning theme_metrics by month if data volume grows

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- Migrations run successfully on dev and staging
- Indices created and query plans using them
- theme_ids in documents table linkable to themes table

FILES TO CREATE:
- migrations/004_add_themes_table.sql
- migrations/005_add_theme_metrics_table.sql
